risks.tef_reason: AiTM phishing (Evilginx/EvilProxy, Tycoon/Mamba PhaaS) is near-continuous commodity traffic against any M365 tenant; 200 harvestable mailboxes and public Entra login make attempts effectively daily (>100/yr).
risks.vuln_reason: Authenticator push is reverse-proxyable and AiTM kits steal the post-MFA session cookie, and with no Conditional Access device-compliance/auth-strength and no SIEM to spot cookie replay, a landed phish reliably becomes a material session takeover (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct loss is IR, forced tenant-wide credential/session resets, and typical BEC vendor-payment fraud, landing in the $100-500k band for a firm this size.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: A hijacked session can bulk-pull SharePoint/OneDrive and SSO-app data, triggering customer notifications, deal loss, and a SOC 2 setback, plausibly $500k-2M.

risks.tef_reason: Internet-facing App Service is hit by automated OWASP/CVE mass scanners and opportunistic exploit attempts continuously (>100/yr); an unauthenticated multi-tenant SaaS endpoint is a standing magnet.
risks.vuln_reason: No WAF and no secure-SDLC owner raise the odds a real SQLi/IDOR/CVE is found, but exploitation-to-material-exfil still depends on an actual flaw existing, putting susceptibility in the 20-45% band rather than near-certain.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct incident-response, forensics, and remediation of an app-layer breach for a ~$20M ARR firm typically falls in the $100-500k band.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Dumping the shared PostgreSQL crosses tenant isolation and exposes ~500k PII across ~1,200 B2B orgs, driving mass breach notification, contractual penalties, churn, and SOC 2 collapse, credibly >$2M.

risks.tef_reason: Infostealer infections of developer laptops and automated repo/history secret-scanning are among the most prevalent 2024-2025 initial-access vectors; with ~60 engineers and historical .env files this company is a realistic frequent target (10-100/yr).
risks.vuln_reason: Long-lived Contributor-scoped service-principal secrets plus committed connection strings mean a single harvested live secret yields full prod access, and with no EDR to catch the stealer and no secret-exposure monitoring, an attempt readily becomes an incident (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: Compromise of the single prod subscription enables exfiltration of the database plus backups and persistence/ransom, with recovery, rebuild, and IR costs in the $500k-2M band.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Loss of production data and backups is a total-breach scenario for all ~500k end users and ~1,200 orgs, driving notification, fines, mass churn, and destroyed SOC 2 credibility, credibly >$2M.

risks.tef_reason: Commodity loaders/infostealers via malvertising, SEO-poisoned installers, and phishing hit remote workers constantly; with ~40% unenrolled laptops and users as local admins, malware reaching a device is a frequent event (10-100/yr).
risks.vuln_reason: Local-admin users, Defender on defaults, no EDR, and no SIEM mean nobody is watching alerts, so a loader can escalate, steal tokens, and encrypt synced SharePoint/OneDrive with high success (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: A 3-person IT team facing multi-day recovery of encrypted synced libraries plus an extortion demand and downtime lands in the $500k-2M band.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Modern affiliates double-extort by stealing data before encryption, so synced corporate/customer data leakage plus operational disruption and reputational hit plausibly reaches $500k-2M.

risks.tef_reason: Dormant-access misuse and credential-stuffing of orphaned accounts are real but lower-volume than external mass campaigns; with manual ticket-driven offboarding and standing contractor Postgres logins, missed revocations creating live dormant access occur a few times a year (1-10/yr).
risks.vuln_reason: Contractor Postgres accounts sit outside SSO/MFA and a missed ticket leaves working prod access indefinitely with no monitoring, so a departed insider or credential-stuffer who targets one has a high chance of undetected material misuse (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct cost of a dormant-credential data export or misuse plus the ensuing IR and forensics falls in the $100-500k band.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Exported customer tables trigger breach notification and churn, and an offboarding failure directly undercuts the in-progress SOC 2 program, plausibly $500k-2M.

risks.tef_reason: Credential phishing against a 200-person, fully remote M365 tenant is near-continuous; AiTM kits like Evilginx are commoditized in phishing-as-a-service offerings, so proxied-login attempts against Entra tenants of this size occur far more than 100 times a year.
risks.vuln_reason: Authenticator push is defeated by AiTM session-cookie theft by design, and with no Conditional Access device-compliance or authentication-strength policies there is no compensating control; a 3-person IT team with no SIEM will not catch the anomalous sign-in, so a user who completes the proxied login is compromised at well above coin-flip probability (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct costs are tenant-wide IR/forensics, mailbox and session remediation across 15 SSO apps, and probable BEC/vendor-payment fraud loss; for a single-identity compromise this typically lands in the $100-500k band, since production Azure infrastructure and the SaaS product itself keep running.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Bulk download of customer PII from SharePoint/SSO-connected apps triggers notification duty to a subset of the 1,200 customer orgs, churn conversations at a $20M ARR base, and a documented identity-control failure mid-SOC 2 that stalls enterprise deals — plausibly $500k-2M but short of a full 500k-user database breach.

risks.tef_reason: Any internet-facing App Service is probed by mass scanners (Shodan-indexed, automated CVE and injection scanning) essentially daily; threat events against this endpoint exceed 100/yr as a baseline of internet exposure.
risks.vuln_reason: Most automated probes fail against any given app, but with no WAF, no dedicated security engineer, no secure-SDLC gates, and a multi-tenant app where one IDOR/SQLi crosses all tenant boundaries, the chance that the sustained probing pressure finds an exploitable flaw and converts to a material incident sits in the 20-45% band.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct loss is emergency IR/forensics, hotfix development, possible emergency downtime of the revenue-generating app, and extortion negotiation — typically $100-500k when the database is read/exfiltrated rather than destroyed.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: A dump of the shared PostgreSQL exposes PII of ~500k end users across all ~1,200 customer orgs: notification at that scale, regulatory exposure, churn and stalled enterprise pipeline against $20M ARR, plus a published extortion sample and a derailed SOC 2 Type I clearly exceed $2M.

risks.tef_reason: With ~60 engineers on unmanaged, local-admin laptops, infostealer campaigns (RedLine/Lumma-class) plus continuous automated scanning of leaked tokens and repo secrets generate on the order of 10-100 credible attempts per year at harvesting a usable GitHub PAT or secret.
risks.vuln_reason: The company confirms live attack paths: historical .env files/connection strings in repo history and long-lived Contributor-scope service-principal secrets in GitHub Actions, with nobody monitoring for secret exposure — a stolen PAT converts to production access at 45-70% because the secrets are valid, long-lived, and unrotated.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Contributor rights over the single production subscription reach App Service, PostgreSQL, and Blob Storage including backups; recovery from a compromise with planted persistence means subscription-wide credential rotation, rebuild, and multi-day SLA-penalized downtime — $500k-2M for a $20M ARR single-product company.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Exfiltration of the production database AND its backups is a full-population breach (500k users, 1,200 orgs) with maximal notification, churn, and deal-loss exposure, and a cloud-governance failure that could reset the SOC 2 timeline — over $2M.

risks.tef_reason: Malvertising, trojanized installers, and commodity phishing hit 200 remote, personal-use-mixed laptops continuously; malware delivery attempts against this workforce comfortably exceed 100 per year.
risks.vuln_reason: Default Defender AV stops much commodity malware, holding per-attempt conversion below half, but ~40% of laptops are outside Intune, users are local admins, and no EDR/SIEM means nobody responds to what gets through — putting escalation to a material incident in the 20-45% band.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Token theft plus encryption of synced SharePoint/OneDrive libraries forces multi-day restoration by a 3-person IT team, halting support, billing, and engineering operations, with an extortion demand on top — $500k-2M in response, recovery, and productivity loss even though the Azure production app itself likely stays up.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Customer data cached in mailboxes and synced libraries creates a notification question for some customer orgs and the incident is reportable to the SOC 2 auditor, but it is not a database-wide breach — churn and reputational cost plausibly land at $100-500k.

risks.tef_reason: A 200-employee company with contractor churn sees dozens of departures a year; with manual ticket-driven offboarding across Entra, 15 SSO apps, Azure, GitHub, and out-of-band Postgres logins, several missed or delayed revocations per year (1-10 threat events) is the realistic base rate.
risks.vuln_reason: Direct contractor Postgres accounts sit outside SSO with no dormant-account review and no detection of anomalous queries, so a surviving credential works indefinitely; still, most departed users never abuse access and credential-stuffing must guess the right account, putting conversion to a material incident at 20-45%.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct loss is investigation and legal response to insider exfiltration via a legitimate credential — no downtime or ransomware recovery — but with no SIEM or query logging, scoping 'what was accessed for how many months' requires expensive external forensics, landing at $100-500k.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: A contractor Postgres login can export multi-tenant customer tables, triggering notification to affected orgs among the 1,200 and churn; an unrevoked-access finding is a classic SOC 2 access-control exception that jeopardizes the Type I audit and pending enterprise deals — $500k-2M, below the full-population breach band.

risks.tef_reason: Credential phishing against a 200-seat M365 tenant is a constant background threat, and Evilginx-class AiTM kits are now standard in commodity phishing-as-a-service (e.g. Tycoon 2FA, EvilProxy); expect tens of AiTM-capable campaigns reaching inboxes per year even if generic spray is near-continuous.
risks.vuln_reason: Authenticator push offers no phishing resistance against session-cookie proxying, and there are no Conditional Access device-compliance or authentication-strength policies to reject the attacker's replayed session; with no SIEM, anomalous sign-ins and inbox rules go unreviewed by a 3-person IT team, so a successful lure very likely matures into a material account takeover (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: Direct losses are incident response, mailbox/session forensics across the tenant, and probable vendor-payment (BEC) fraud, which for a company this size typically lands in the $100-500k band rather than causing production downtime.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: A single compromised identity fronts SharePoint/OneDrive and ~15 SSO apps with no data-loss controls, so bulk download of customer PII is plausible, triggering notification to affected customer orgs, churn among the 1,200 tenants, and a SOC 2 Type I setback ($500k-2M).

risks.tef_reason: The App Service is internet-facing with no WAF, so OWASP-class probing and mass CVE scanning (e.g. post-disclosure framework exploits) hit it near-continuously — well over 100 threat events per year is the norm for any public multi-tenant SaaS endpoint.
risks.vuln_reason: Absent a WAF, secure-SDLC gates, or a security engineer driving patch SLAs, an exploitable flaw would go unshielded and exploitation undetected (no SIEM, no app-layer alerting); however, each individual scan only becomes a material incident if a matching injectable endpoint or unpatched CVE actually exists, keeping per-attempt susceptibility in the 5-20% band.
risks.primary_impact_reason: A tenant-crossing dump of the shared PostgreSQL database forces full forensic IR, emergency patching, extortion handling, and likely external counsel/DFIR retainers at a firm with no in-house security staff — realistically $500k-2M direct.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Exfiltration of PII for ~500k end users requires breach notification across ~1,200 customer B2B orgs, invites regulatory exposure and contractual penalties, and would gut the in-progress SOC 2 program and pipeline deals — plausibly exceeding $2M against $20M ARR.

risks.tef_reason: With ~60 engineers on partly unmanaged, local-admin laptops, infostealer compromise of a GitHub PAT or session token (RedLine/Lumma-class, the same pattern behind the 2023-24 wave of repo-secret breaches) is a realistic 1-10 times-per-year event, supplemented by opportunistic mining of leaked repo history.
risks.vuln_reason: The control environment converts one stolen token into subscription compromise: historical .env connection strings persist in repo history, the CI service principal holds long-lived Contributor secrets on the single production subscription, and there is no secret scanning, rotation regime, or SIEM to catch anomalous SP sign-ins — 45-70% that a harvested credential becomes a material incident.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Contributor rights over the only production subscription reach App Service, PostgreSQL, and Blob Storage including backups, so direct cost spans full-environment IR, credential/infrastructure rebuild, and potential backup destruction or extortion — $500k-2M.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Exfiltration of the production database plus backups is a worst-case disclosure of all ~500k end users' PII, driving notification to all 1,200 customer orgs, fines, mass churn, and derailment of the SOC 2 Type I audit — likely >$2M.

risks.tef_reason: 200 remote-first employees mixing personal browsing with work on their laptops face commodity malware delivery (malvertising, trojanized installers, phishing loaders) on the order of 10-100 meaningful attempts per year across the fleet.
risks.vuln_reason: Default Defender AV blocks most commodity payloads, but ~40% of laptops are outside Intune, users run as local admin enabling AV tampering and privilege escalation, and with no EDR or SIEM nobody triages the alerts that do fire — so 20-45% of determined attempts progress to a material foothold or encryption event.
risks.primary_impact_reason: Encryption of synced SharePoint libraries via the OneDrive client plus laptop rebuilds means multi-day operational disruption and an extortion demand, but SharePoint versioning/recycle-bin recovery and the endpoint-centric blast radius keep direct cost around $100-500k for a 3-person IT team.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Customer-facing support and delivery delays cause some churn and the incident weakens the SOC 2 narrative, but unless stolen Entra tokens are leveraged into bulk data theft (scored under R1/R3 paths), notification obligations are limited — $100-500k.

risks.tef_reason: Normal attrition at a 200-person company plus contractor churn produces dormant credentials many times per year, and a subset become threat events via disgruntled-leaver misuse or credential-stuffing of unwatched accounts — realistically 1-10 threat events annually.
risks.vuln_reason: Offboarding is manual tickets across Entra, ~15 SSO apps, Azure, GitHub, and standing Postgres logins that sit outside SSO/MFA entirely, and there is no access recertification, database activity monitoring, or SIEM — so a misuse attempt against a surviving credential succeeds and goes unnoticed in most cases (45-70%).
risks.primary_impact_reason: A direct Postgres account lets a former contractor export customer tables wholesale; direct cost is forensic reconstruction of months of unlogged access, legal action, and emergency credential audits across all systems — $100-500k.
risks.secondary_impact_reason: Because contractor DB accounts likely see multi-tenant data, an export triggers breach notification to a broad slice of the 1,200 customer orgs, and a demonstrably failed offboarding control is a direct SOC 2 Type I finding that jeopardizes the audit timeline and enterprise deals ($500k-2M).

per_lens.primary_impact_reason: Direct loss is investigation and legal response to insider exfiltration via a legitimate credential — no downtime or ransomware recovery — but with no SIEM or query logging, scoping 'what was accessed for how many months' requires expensive external forensics, landing at $100-500k.
per_lens.secondary_impact_reason: A contractor Postgres login can export multi-tenant customer tables, triggering notification to affected orgs among the 1,200 and churn; an unrevoked-access finding is a classic SOC 2 access-control exception that jeopardizes the Type I audit and pending enterprise deals — $500k-2M, below the full-population breach band.

per_lens.tef_reason: Normal attrition at a 200-person company plus contractor churn produces dormant credentials many times per year, and a subset become threat events via disgruntled-leaver misuse or credential-stuffing of unwatched accounts — realistically 1-10 threat events annually.
per_lens.vuln_reason: Offboarding is manual tickets across Entra, ~15 SSO apps, Azure, GitHub, and standing Postgres logins that sit outside SSO/MFA entirely, and there is no access recertification, database activity monitoring, or SIEM — so a misuse attempt against a surviving credential succeeds and goes unnoticed in most cases (45-70%).
per_lens.primary_impact_reason: A direct Postgres account lets a former contractor export customer tables wholesale; direct cost is forensic reconstruction of months of unlogged access, legal action, and emergency credential audits across all systems — $100-500k.
per_lens.secondary_impact_reason: Because contractor DB accounts likely see multi-tenant data, an export triggers breach notification to a broad slice of the 1,200 customer orgs, and a demonstrably failed offboarding control is a direct SOC 2 Type I finding that jeopardizes the audit timeline and enterprise deals ($500k-2M).

ranking.title: Leaked CI/CD and cloud secrets leading to Azure production subscription compromise
AiTM credential phishing leading to M365/Entra account takeover
Public web app exploitation (SQLi/IDOR/CVE) leading to PostgreSQL customer-data exfiltration
Offboarding gaps: dormant employee/contractor access to production data
